analogy: Think of "Social Movements" through the chapter's own opening images: A great many students and office-workers around the world go to work only for five or six days and rest on the weekends. Yet, very few people who relax on their day off realise that this holiday is the outcome of a long struggle by... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Social Movements is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that a great many students and office-workers around the world go to work only for five or six days and rest on the weekends.

The text explains that yet, very few people who relax on their day off realise that this holiday is the outcome of a long struggle by workers.

The text explains that that the work-day should not exceed eight hours, that men and women should be paid equally for doing the same work, that workers are entitled to social security and pension — these and many other rights were gained through social movements.

The text explains that social movements have shaped the world we live in and continue to do so.

The text explains that we often assume that the rights we enjoy just happened to exist.

The text explains that it is important to recall the struggles of the past, which made these rights possible.

The text explains that you have read about the 19th century social reform movements, of the struggles against caste and gender discrimination and of the nationalist movement in India that brought us independence from colonial rule in 1947.

The text explains that you are familiar also with the many nationalist movements around the world in Asia, Africa and Americas that put an end to colonial rule.

The text explains that the socialist movements world over, the civil rights movement in the United States in the 1950s and 1960s that fought for equal rights for Blacks, the anti-apartheid struggle in South Africa have all changed the world in fundamental ways.

The text explains that social movements not The Right to Vote Universal adult franchise, or the right of every adult to vote, is one of the foremost rights guaranteed by the Indian Constitution.

The text explains that it means that we cannot be governed by anyone other than the people we have ourselves elected to represent us.

The text explains that this right is a radical departure from the days of colonial rule when ordinary people were forced to submit to the authority of colonial officers who represented the interests of the British Crown.

The text explains that however, even in Britain, not everyone was allowed to vote.

The text explains that voting rights were limited to property-owning men.

The text explains that chartism was a social movement for parliamentary representation in England.

The text explains that in 1839, more than 1.25 million people signed the People’s Charter asking for universal male suffrage, voting by ballot, and the right to stand for elections without owning property.

The text explains that such action is often directed against the state and takes the form of demanding changes in state policy or practice.

The text explains that social movements are directed towards some specific goals.

The text explains that people left out by the formal political system join social movements or non-party political formations in order to put pressure on the state from outside.

The text explains that over the decades there has been a great deal of concern about the unchecked use of natural resources and a model of development that creates new needs that further demands greater exploitation of the already depleted natural resources.

The text explains that thus big dams displace people from their homes and sources of livelihood.

The text explains that it was observed that some of the villages most affected by landslides lay directly below forests where felling operations had taken place…. …The villagers’ cause was taken up by the Dashauli Gram Swaraja Sangh (DGSS), a cooperative organisation based in Chamoli district. …Despite these early protests, the government went ahead with the yearly auction of forests in November.

The text explains that for instance, the Bardoli Satyagraha (1928, Surat District) a ‘non-tax’ campaign as part of the nationwide non-cooperation movement, a campaign of refusal to pay land revenue and the Champaran Satyagraha (1917–18) directed against indigo plantations.

The text explains that the second was directed against the feudal conditions in the princely state of Hyderabad and was led by the CPI.

The text explains that they soon occupied land in the name of peasants’ committees, burnt all land records ‘which had been used to cheat them of their dues’, cancelled all hypothecary debts, passed death sentences on oppressive landlords, formed armed bands by looting guns from landlords, armed themselves with conventional weapons like bows, arrows and spears, and set up parallel administration to look after the villages… Source: Sumanata Banerjee “Naxalbari and the Left Movement” in ed.

The text explains that there were waves of strikes in the textile mills in Bombay.

The text explains that most of the benefits from the mining and industrial projects in this mineral-rich region had gone to dikus even as adivasi lands had been alienated.

The text explains that 2004. ‘The Working Class Movement in India : Trade Unions and the State’ in Manoranjan Mohanti Class, Caste and Gender.

The text explains that the Indian Women’s Movement: Reform and Revival.

The text explains that in Yogendra Singh (Ed.), Indian Sociology: Emerging Concepts, Structure and Change.

The text explains that veena Das, The Oxford India Companion to Sociology and Social Anthropology. pp.

The text explains that 2002. “Chipko: Social History of an Environmental Movement” in Shah Ghansyam Social Movements and the State.

The text explains that ethnicity and Gender: Select Essays on Tribes.

The text explains that kinship Terminology and Marriage Rules: The Khasis of North- East India.

The text explains that nation, Civil Society and Social Movements: Essays in Political Sociology.

The text explains that 2004. ‘Dalit Women Talk Differently: A Critique of ‘Difference’ and Towards a Dalit Feminist Standpoint Position’ in Maitrayee Chaudhuri Ed.

The text explains that writing Caste/Writing Gender: Narrating Dalit Women’s Testimonies.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India, Chapter 8: Social Movements

schemes.name: Such
schemes.point: Such action is often directed against the state and takes the form of demanding changes in state policy or practice. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch8 pg 2)

schemes.name: Collective
schemes.point: Collective action must be marked by some degree of organisation. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch8 pg 2)

schemes.name: This
schemes.point: This organisation may include a leadership and a structure that defines how members relate to each other, make decisions and carry them out. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch8 pg 2)

schemes.name: Social
schemes.point: Social movements also chart out campaigns that include lobbying with the government, media and other important makers of public opinion. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch8 pg 3)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 8.1 Features oF a Social Movement and 8.6 Caste B ased MoveMents as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Social Movements; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Social Movements through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 8.1 Features oF a Social Movement and 8.6 Caste B ased MoveMents to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
